D6 Display L4 Analyze Reference

E-Ink: Particles, Not Pixels

an e-reader page is a layer of microcapsules — charged white and black flakes that a field pulls to the surface and then holds without power

Electrophoretic ink is a sheet of clear microcapsules, each holding positively charged white pigment and negatively charged black pigment in oil. A voltage across the capsule drags one colour to the viewer's side; reverse it and the other rises. With no backlight, you read by reflected light — and once set, the image holds with the power off.

Sweep the drive voltage and watch the pigments migrate inside each capsule, the page surface shifting from black through grey to white. Set it to zero from any point — the particles stay put. That bistability is why e-ink sips power.
